These cookies do not store any personally identifiable data. Cookie PHPSESSID Duration session Description This cookie is native to PHP applications. The cookie is used to store and identify a users' unique session ID for the purpose of managing user session on the website. The cookie is a session cookies and is deleted when all the browser windows are closed. Cookie m Duration 1 year 1 month 4 days Description No description available. Cookie __stripe_sid Duration 30 minutes Description Stripe sets this cookie cookie to process payments. Cookie cookieyes-consent Duration 1 year Description CookieYes sets this cookie to remember users' consent preferences so that their preferences are respected on their subsequent visits to this site. It does not collect or store any personal information of the site visitors. Cookie JSESSIONID Duration session Description The JSESSIONID cookie is used by New Relic to store a session identifier so that New Relic can monitor session counts for an application. Functional Functional cookies help perform certain functionalities like sharing the content of the website on social media platforms, collecting feedback, and other third-party features. Cookie loglevel Duration never Description No description available. Cookie attribution_user_id Duration 1 year Description This cookie is set by Typeform for usage statistics and is used in context with the website's pop-up questionnaires and messengering. Cookie __cf_bm Duration 30 minutes Description This cookie, set by Cloudflare, is used to support Cloudflare Bot Management. Analytics Analytical cookies are used to understand how visitors interact with the website. These cookies help provide information on metrics such as the number of visitors, bounce rate, traffic source, etc. Cookie _hjSessionUser_3066330 Duration 1 year Description No description Cookie _hjIncludedInSessionSample_3066330 Duration 2 minutes Description No description Cookie _hjSession_3066330 Duration 30 minutes Description No description Cookie _hjFirstSeen Duration 30 minutes Description Hotjar sets this cookie to identify a new user’s first session. It stores a true/false value, indicating whether it was the first time Hotjar saw this user. Cookie _hjAbsoluteSessionInProgress Duration 30 minutes Description Hotjar sets this cookie to detect the first pageview session of a user. This is a True/False flag set by the cookie. Cookie _gid Duration 1 day Description Installed by Google Analytics, _gid cookie stores information on how visitors use a website, while also creating an analytics report of the website's performance. Some of the data that are collected include the number of visitors, their source, and the pages they visit anonymously. Cookie _gat_UA-* Duration 1 minute Description Google Analytics sets this cookie for user behaviour tracking. Cookie _ga_* Duration 1 year 1 month 4 days Description Google Analytics sets this cookie to store and count page views. Cookie _ga Duration 1 year 1 month 4 days Description The _ga cookie, installed by Google Analytics, calculates visitor, session and campaign data and also keeps track of site usage for the site's analytics report. The cookie stores information anonymously and assigns a randomly generated number to recognize unique visitors. Cookie _buzzsprout_session Duration session Description No description Cookie visitor-id Duration 1 year Description No description available. Cookie _hjRecordingLastActivity Duration never Description Hotjar sets this cookie when a user recording starts and when data is sent through the WebSocket. Cookie _hjRecordingEnabled Duration never Description Hotjar sets this cookie when a Recording starts and is read when the recording module is initialized, to see if the user is already in a recording in a particular session. Cookie _hjTLDTest Duration session Description To determine the most generic cookie path that has to be used instead of the page hostname, Hotjar sets the _hjTLDTest cookie to store different URL substring alternatives until it fails. Cookie UID Duration 1 year 1 month 4 days Description Scorecard Research sets this cookie for browser behaviour research. Cookie CONSENT Duration 2 years Description YouTube sets this cookie via embedded youtube-videos and registers anonymous statistical data. Cookie vuid Duration 1 year 1 month 4 days Description Vimeo installs this cookie to collect tracking information by setting a unique ID to embed videos to the website. Cookie _omappvp Duration 1 year 1 month 4 days Description The _omappvp cookie is set to distinguish new and returning users and is used in conjunction with _omappvs cookie. Cookie _omappvs Duration 20 minutes Description The _omappvs cookie, used in conjunction with the _omappvp cookies, is used to determine if the visitor has visited the website before, or if it is a new visitor. Performance Performance cookies are used to understand and analyze the key performance indexes of the website which helps in delivering a better user experience for the visitors. Cookie _gat Duration 1 minute Description This cookie is installed by Google Universal Analytics to restrain request rate and thus limit the collection of data on high traffic sites. Advertisement Advertisement cookies are used to provide visitors with customized advertisements based on the pages you visited previously and to analyze the effectiveness of the ad campaigns. Cookie tf_respondent_cc Duration 6 months Description No description Cookie rl_page_init_referrer Duration never Description Rudderstack sets this cookie, which is used to store performed actions on the website. Cookie rl_user_id Duration never Description RudderStack set this cookie to store a unique user ID for the Marketing/Tracking purpose. Cookie rl_page_init_referring_domain Duration never Description Rudderstack sets this cookie, which is used to store performed actions on the website. Cookie rl_group_trait Duration never Description Rudderstack sets this cookie, which is used to store performed actions on the website. Cookie rl_group_id Duration never Description Rudderstack sets this cookie, which is used to store performed actions on the website. Cookie rl_trait Duration never Description Rudderstack sets this cookie, which is used to store performed actions on the website. Cookie rl_anonymous_id Duration never Description RudderStack set this cookie to store statistical data of users' behaviour on the website, which can be used for internal analytics by the website operator. Cookie test_cookie Duration 15 minutes Description The test_cookie is set by doubleclick.net and is used to determine if the user's browser supports cookies. Cookie YSC Duration session Description YSC cookie is set by Youtube and is used to track the views of embedded videos on Youtube pages. Cookie VISITOR_INFO1_LIVE Duration 5 months 27 days Description A cookie set by YouTube to measure bandwidth that determines whether the user gets the new or old player interface. Cookie yt-remote-device-id Duration never Description YouTube sets this cookie to store the video preferences of the user using embedded YouTube video. Cookie yt-remote-connected-devices Duration never Description YouTube sets this cookie to store the video preferences of the user using embedded YouTube video. Cookie yt.innertube::requests Duration never Description This cookie, set by YouTube, registers a unique ID to store data on what videos from YouTube the user has seen. Cookie yt.innertube::nextId Duration never Description This cookie, set by YouTube, registers a unique ID to store data on what videos from YouTube the user has seen. Cookie IDE Duration 1 year 24 days Description Google DoubleClick IDE cookies store information about how the user uses the website to present them with relevant ads according to the user profile. Uncategorized Other uncategorized cookies are those that are being analyzed and have not been classified into a category as yet. No cookies to display. Reject All Save My Preferences Accept All

Skip to content Suggested Topics: Home Best in Biotech Eight allergy companies to watch in 2026 Photo credits: Allef Vinicius (Unsplash) Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page " * " indicates required fields The idea of developing treatments for allergies is gaining momentum in the biotech industry. Once dominated by antihistamines, EpiPens, and general avoidance strategies, the field is now being reshaped by disease-modifying approaches, such as immunotherapy, monoclonal antibodies, and next-generation vaccines. This shift comes at a time when allergic conditions are rising globally, driven by environmental factors, urbanisation, and changing lifestyles. As a result, several companies have emerged to develop allergy therapies that go beyond emergency management, instead offering long-term protection or even desensitisation. In this article, we look at eight allergy companies leading the way in 2026. Table of contents Allergy Therapeutics Aravax DBV Technologies IgGenix Inimmune Intrommune Therapeutics Mabylon Stallergenes Greer Allergy immunotherapies: A market set for significant growth Allergy Therapeutics Back to Top Technology: Immunotherapy and vaccines Lead product: Grassmuno for grass pollen allergy Recent news: Grassumo received marketing authorization in Germany U.K.-based biotech Allergy Therapeutics is focused on diagnosing and developing treatments for allergic disorders, including aluminium-free immunotherapies that have the potential to cure disease. The organization also operates by selling proprietary and third-party products from its subsidiaries in nine major European countries and via distribution agreements in an additional 10 countries. Allergy Therapeutics was recently granted marketing authorization in Germany for its subcutaneous grass pollen allergen immunotherapy, Grassmuno. It works through a course of six injections, which are given before the hay fever season starts to reduce moderate to severe grass pollen allergy symptoms. According to the company, Grassmuno has the advantage of being a short course of injections, whereas other subcutaneous immunotherapy products generally require months-long treatment schedules. The company is also developing VLP Peanut, a short-course peanut allergy vaccine candidate administered via subcutaneous injection. A phase 1/2a trial of the asset met its primary safety endpoint with an excellent safety profile up to the highest planned dose; preliminary data from 48 participants demonstrated that a 2000-fold increase in the dose of VLP Peanut was safe and well tolerated. Biomarker data in peanut allergy patients also demonstrated a strong immunomodulating response. According to the company, plans are now underway to prepare an investigational medicinal product for a phase 2b trial, with dosing of VLP Peanut being informed by the combined safety and efficacy information generated during the phase 1/2a trial. Aravax Back to Top Technology: Immunotherapy Lead candidate: PVX108 for peanut allergy Recent news: Has strengthened its board in preparation for taking PVX108 into phase 3 Dedicated to revolutionising the treatment of food allergies by developing next-generation, disease-modifying immunotherapies that are safer and more convenient than existing approaches, Aravax is currently focused on the development of its lead candidate, PVX108. The asset is an advanced immunotherapy designed to “re-train” the immune system by administering engineered peptides to precisely target T cells and reverse the course of allergic disease. Additionally, unlike most other treatments, PVX108 does not contain peanut proteins, which can potentially put patients at significant risk of serious side effects, leading to complex and burdensome dosing regimens. Suggested Articles Battling food allergies: emerging immunotherapies 10 immunotherapy companies making waves in 2023 Eight monoclonal antibody biotechs making strides in the clinic Six bispecific antibody companies you should know about Monoclonal antibodies: are they the key to treating Alzheimer’s disease? Since closing a $42 million series B round in January 2024, Aravax has been progressing PVX108 through phase 2 of development. In October that same year, the allergy company announced that it had completed recruitment in its phase 2 trial of the candidate. The study, conducted under a U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) Investigational New Drug Application, enrolled 95 patients across eight trial sites in the U.S. and six in Australia. Trial participants were children and adolescents aged four to 17 years old with a peanut allergy. In phase 1 trials, researchers observed that PVX108 produced relevant changes in the immune response to peanut protein, which continued to develop once dosing had completed. Meanwhile, the primary endpoint of the phase 2 study is the ratio of the maximum tolerated dose of peanut protein in a controlled food challenge at the end of the study period relative to baseline. Headline results are expected in H1 2026, and the company has strengthened its board in recent times in preparation for taking PVX108 into phase 3. DBV Technologies Back to Top Technology: Epicutaneous immunotherapy Lead candidate: VIASKIN peanut patch for peanut allergy Recent news: Announced positive topline results from phase 3 trial of VIASKIN peanut patch French biotech company DBV Technologies is advancing epicutaneous immunotherapy – which aims to re-educate the immune system of patients through the skin – using its VIASKIN patch technology to develop treatment options for immunologic diseases with significant unmet medical needs. The company currently has a pipeline of allergy candidates that it says could potentially transform the care of food-allergic patients. The company’s most advanced product candidate is the VIASKIN peanut patch. In December 2025, DBV announced positive topline results from a phase 3 trial of the asset in peanut-allergic children aged four to seven years. The study met its primary endpoint as the patch demonstrated a statistically significant treatment effect, with 46.6% of children in the VIASKIN Peanut arm meeting the treatment responder criteria after 12 months, as compared to 14.8% of children in the placebo arm, exceeding the lower bound prespecified threshold of 15%. DBV is now moving forward with plans for a Biologics License Application (BLA) submission in the U.S. in the first half of this year. In March 2025, the allergy company announced a financing of up to $306.9 million (€284.5 million), which it said at the time would go toward advancing the VIASKIN peanut patch through BLA submission and U.S. commercial launch if the product is approved. IgGenix Back to Top Technology: IgG antibodies Lead candidate: IGNX001 for peanut allergy Recent news: Completed phase 1 trial of IGNX001 With a single-cell RNA-sequencing-based discovery platform called SEQ SIFTER, IgGenix captures extremely rare human B cells that produce IgE antibodies directly from human blood. Through its technology, the company then isolates and re-engineers fully human, high-affinity, allergen-specific IgG antibodies designed to block and prevent the allergic cascade. By targeting common immunodominant antigens and their most immunodominant epitopes across patients, IgGenix intends to treat both pediatric and adult patients. The allergy company’s lead candidate, IGNX001, is a human IgG4 monoclonal antibody being developed for peanut allergies and is designed to neutralize the most clinically important peanut allergens and epitopes. The company completed enrollment in a phase 1 trial of the candidate in September 2025. According to the NIH clinical trials website , the study was completed in November 2025 – although IgGenix has not yet posted the results. The company also has two more allergy programs in its pipeline for undisclosed indications in preclinical stages. Inimmune Back to Top Technology: Immunotherapy Lead candidate: INI-2004 for allergic rhinitis Recent news: Completed enrollment and dosed first patient in phase 2 study of INI-2004 Inimmune’s focus is to discover and develop disease-modifying immunotherapies. It currently has a pipeline of two clinical-stage Toll-like receptor (TLR) agonists in both immuno-oncology and allergy. The company’s allergy therapy, INI-2004, is its most advanced candidate. INI-2004 is an allergen-agnostic immunotherapy that binds and activates toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4). Inimmune announced in March that it had completed enrollment and dosed the first patient in a phase 2 trial of the candidate in patients with allergic rhinitis. In a prior phase 1 study, in response to the ragweed challenge, participants treated intranasally with INI-2004 showed a dose-dependent improvement in nasal congestion as measured by acoustic rhinometry compared to placebo participants. Topline data from the phase 2 trial is expected in June 2026. The company is also investigating INI-2004 in food allergies via a 2024 collaboration with Intrommune, which is evaluating whether the addition of INI-2004 to Intrommune’s INT301 can enhance the desensitization response in preclinical models for peanut allergies. According to the press release, the combination of these two approaches is expected to result in the rapid and long-term relief of allergic symptoms resulting from peanut exposure and would be a game-changer for patients suffering from this allergy. Intrommune Therapeutics Back to Top Technology: Oral mucosal immunotherapy Lead candidate: Multifood INT302 Recent news: Announced an independent editorial highlighting positive phase 1 safety data for INT302 Intrommune’s core technology enables immunotherapeutic agents to be delivered in a specially formulated toothpaste designed to incorporate and stabilize allergenic proteins, representing a novel approach for the treatment of peanut and other food allergies. Conducting allergy immunotherapy via teeth brushing is referred to as oral mucosal immunotherapy (OMIT). This delivers allergenic proteins to areas of the oral cavity, potentially driving the immune system toward tolerance without ingesting the allergen. Specifically designed to help those who suffer from food allergies, the company’s lead asset is called Multifood INT302. The product is intended to significantly raise a patient’s immune threshold through daily use of OMIT toothpaste beyond what has triggered a potentially dangerous allergic reaction via accidental exposure. This additional protection has the potential to relieve the persistent anxiety that allergic individuals often feel toward accidental exposure. In May last year, Intrommune announced that there had been an independent editorial highlighting the safety data for INT302. In a phase 1 trial, the candidate met all of its primary and secondary endpoints, achieving exceptional safety and tolerability, with no moderate or severe systemic reactions or anaphylaxis reported in treated participants. Furthermore, as mentioned above, Intrommune entered into a collaboration with Inimmune to test the combination of INT302 and Inimmune’s INI-2004 in preclinical models for peanut allergies. Mabylon Back to Top Technology: Multispecific antibodies Lead candidate: MY006 for peanut allergy Recent news: Initiated a phase 1a/b trial for MY006 Swiss biotech company Mabylon has a technology platform that leverages human-derived antibodies sourced from patients’ immune repertoires to develop multispecific therapeutics targeting allergies, inflammatory disorders, and neurodegenerative diseases. The company’s lead candidate, MY006, is a trispecific monoclonal antibody and is intended for the treatment of peanut allergy. By binding to critical allergenic epitopes, MY006 works by neutralizing major peanut allergens and is designed to prevent the immune activation responsible for anaphylaxis. It is being developed as a novel alternative to desensitization approaches, with the potential to offer protection without allergen exposure and long-lasting efficacy with only a few subcutaneous injections per year. After raising CHF 30 million ($37 million) in August 2025 to further develop MY006, the allergy company initiated a phase 1a/b trial for the candidate in December. Mabylon also has two other allergy candidates in preclinical development: MY010, a multispecific and cross-reactive antibody for birch pollen allergy that will soon be entering Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls (CMC) and IND-enabling studies, and MY011, a broadly cross-reactive antibody-based therapeutic for grass pollen allergy. The study, conducted under a U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) Investigational New Drug Application, enrolled 95 patients across eight trial sites in the U.S. and six in Australia. Trial participants were children and adolescents aged four to 17 years old with a peanut allergy. In phase 1 trials, researchers observed that PVX108 produced relevant changes in the immune response to peanut protein, which continued to develop once dosing had completed. Meanwhile, the primary endpoint of the phase 2 study is the ratio of the maximum tolerated dose of peanut protein in a controlled food challenge at the end of the study period relative to baseline. Headline results are expected in H1 2026, and the company has strengthened its board in recent times in preparation for taking PVX108 into phase 3. DBV Technologies Back to Top Technology: Epicutaneous immunotherapy Lead candidate: VIASKIN peanut patch for peanut allergy Recent news: Announced positive topline results from phase 3 trial of VIASKIN peanut patch French biotech company DBV Technologies is advancing epicutaneous immunotherapy – which aims to re-educate the immune system of patients through the skin – using its VIASKIN patch technology to develop treatment options for immunologic diseases with significant unmet medical needs. In March 2025, the allergy company announced a financing of up to $306.9 million (€284.5 million), which it said at the time would go toward advancing the VIASKIN peanut patch through BLA submission and U.S. commercial launch if the product is approved. Through its technology, the company then isolates and re-engineers fully human, high-affinity, allergen-specific IgG antibodies designed to block and prevent the allergic cascade. By targeting common immunodominant antigens and their most immunodominant epitopes across patients, IgGenix intends to treat both pediatric and adult patients. The allergy company’s lead candidate, IGNX001, is a human IgG4 monoclonal antibody being developed for peanut allergies and is designed to neutralize the most clinically important peanut allergens and epitopes. The company completed enrollment in a phase 1 trial of the candidate in September 2025. According to the NIH clinical trials website , the study was completed in November 2025 – although IgGenix has not yet posted the results. Inimmune Back to Top Technology: Immunotherapy Lead candidate: INI-2004 for allergic rhinitis Recent news: Completed enrollment and dosed first patient in phase 2 study of INI-2004 Inimmune’s focus is to discover and develop disease-modifying immunotherapies. It currently has a pipeline of two clinical-stage Toll-like receptor (TLR) agonists in both immuno-oncology and allergy. The company’s allergy therapy, INI-2004, is its most advanced candidate. INI-2004 is an allergen-agnostic immunotherapy that binds and activates toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4). Inimmune announced in March that it had completed enrollment and dosed the first patient in a phase 2 trial of the candidate in patients with allergic rhinitis. In a prior phase 1 study, in response to the ragweed challenge, participants treated intranasally with INI-2004 showed a dose-dependent improvement in nasal congestion as measured by acoustic rhinometry compared to placebo participants. Topline data from the phase 2 trial is expected in June 2026. The company is also investigating INI-2004 in food allergies via a 2024 collaboration with Intrommune, which is evaluating whether the addition of INI-2004 to Intrommune’s INT301 can enhance the desensitization response in preclinical models for peanut allergies. According to the press release, the combination of these two approaches is expected to result in the rapid and long-term relief of allergic symptoms resulting from peanut exposure and would be a game-changer for patients suffering from this allergy. Intrommune Therapeutics Back to Top Technology: Oral mucosal immunotherapy Lead candidate: Multifood INT302 Recent news: Announced an independent editorial highlighting positive phase 1 safety data for INT302 Intrommune’s core technology enables immunotherapeutic agents to be delivered in a specially formulated toothpaste designed to incorporate and stabilize allergenic proteins, representing a novel approach for the treatment of peanut and other food allergies. Conducting allergy immunotherapy via teeth brushing is referred to as oral mucosal immunotherapy (OMIT). This delivers allergenic proteins to areas of the oral cavity, potentially driving the immune system toward tolerance without ingesting the allergen. Specifically designed to help those who suffer from food allergies, the company’s lead asset is called Multifood INT302. The product is intended to significantly raise a patient’s immune threshold through daily use of OMIT toothpaste beyond what has triggered a potentially dangerous allergic reaction via accidental exposure. This additional protection has the potential to relieve the persistent anxiety that allergic individuals often feel toward accidental exposure. In May last year, Intrommune announced that there had been an independent editorial highlighting the safety data for INT302. In a phase 1 trial, the candidate met all of its primary and secondary endpoints, achieving exceptional safety and tolerability, with no moderate or severe systemic reactions or anaphylaxis reported in treated participants. Furthermore, as mentioned above, Intrommune entered into a collaboration with Inimmune to test the combination of INT302 and Inimmune’s INI-2004 in preclinical models for peanut allergies. Mabylon Back to Top Technology: Multispecific antibodies Lead candidate: MY006 for peanut allergy Recent news: Initiated a phase 1a/b trial for MY006 Swiss biotech company Mabylon has a technology platform that leverages human-derived antibodies sourced from patients’ immune repertoires to develop multispecific therapeutics targeting allergies, inflammatory disorders, and neurodegenerative diseases. The company’s lead candidate, MY006, is a trispecific monoclonal antibody and is intended for the treatment of peanut allergy. By binding to critical allergenic epitopes, MY006 works by neutralizing major peanut allergens and is designed to prevent the immune activation responsible for anaphylaxis. It is being developed as a novel alternative to desensitization approaches, with the potential to offer protection without allergen exposure and long-lasting efficacy with only a few subcutaneous injections per year. After raising CHF 30 million ($37 million) in August 2025 to further develop MY006, the allergy company initiated a phase 1a/b trial for the candidate in December. Mabylon also has two other allergy candidates in preclinical development: MY010, a multispecific and cross-reactive antibody for birch pollen allergy that will soon be entering Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls (CMC) and IND-enabling studies, and MY011, a broadly cross-reactive antibody-based therapeutic for grass pollen allergy. Stallergenes Greer Back to Top Technology: Immunotherapy Approved products: Staloral, Oralair, Actair, Palforzia, etc. Recent news: Formed partnership with Nuance Pharma to develop Actair in China With more than 120 years of expertise in allergies and allergen immunotherapy, Stallergenes Greer is very much a leader in the field. The company operates internationally and supports patients and healthcare professionals with both diagnostic tools and therapeutic solutions. The core of Stallergenes Greer’s work lies in allergen immunotherapy, which involves administering controlled doses of specific allergens to gradually train the immune system to tolerate them. It uses this approach to treat respiratory allergies (such as pollen or dust mites), food allergies, and even venom allergies. The company’s treatments are available in multiple forms, including sublingual tablets or drops, subcutaneous injections, and oral immunotherapy, reflecting a personalised medicine approach tailored to individual patient needs. The allergy company has several approved and widely used products within its portfolio. These include sublingual therapies such as Staloral, Oralair (also known as Aitgrys), and Actair (Aitmyte/Orylmyte), as well as injectable products like Alustal and Albey. It has also developed Palforzia, an oral immunotherapy for peanut allergy. Together, these products span diagnosis and treatment, forming a comprehensive range of solutions aimed at improving long-term outcomes and quality of life for people living with allergies. Stallergenes Greer has formed several partnerships over the years; one of its most recent ones was with Chinese company Nuance Pharma in September last year, in which the two companies said they would work together on the development and registration of Actair in China. Allergy immunotherapies: A market set for significant growth Back to Top The global allergy immunotherapy market size, which was valued at $1.8 billion in 2023, is projected to reach $4.9 billion by 2033, growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 10.4% from 2024 to 2033. This is largely attributed to an increase in the prevalence of allergies, along with advancements in technology and a surge in awareness of long-term treatment benefits. Indeed, big pharma is starting to take note of just how beneficial allergy therapies could be: in January, GSK paid $2.2 billion to acquire Rapt Therapeutics and its IgE antibody to protect against food allergy reactions, and Novartis agreed to pay up to $2 billion just last month to acquire Excellergy and its anti-IgE antibody that could be a successor to the blockbuster Xolair. In May last year, Intrommune announced that there had been an independent editorial highlighting the safety data for INT302. Allergy immunotherapies: A market set for significant growth Back to Top The global allergy immunotherapy market size, which was valued at $1.8 billion in 2023, is projected to reach $4.9 billion by 2033, growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 10.4% from 2024 to 2033. This is largely attributed to an increase in the prevalence of allergies, along with advancements in technology and a surge in awareness of long-term treatment benefits. Indeed, big pharma is starting to take note of just how beneficial allergy therapies could be: in January, GSK paid $2.2 billion to acquire Rapt Therapeutics and its IgE antibody to protect against food allergy reactions, and Novartis agreed to pay up to $2 billion just last month to acquire Excellergy and its anti-IgE antibody that could be a successor to the blockbuster Xolair.
The idea of developing treatments for allergies is gaining momentum in the biotech industry. Once dominated by antihistamines, EpiPens, and general avoidance strategies, the field is now being reshaped by disease-modifying approaches, such as immunotherapy, monoclonal antibodies, and next-generation vaccines. This shift comes at a time when allergic conditions are rising globally, driven by environmental factors, urbanisation, and changing lifestyles. As a result, several companies have emerged to develop allergy therapies that go beyond emergency management, instead offering long-term protection or even desensitisation. In this article, we look at eight allergy companies leading the way in 2026.

U.K.-based biotech Allergy Therapeutics is focused on diagnosing and developing treatments for allergic disorders, including aluminium-free immunotherapies that have the potential to cure disease. The organization also operates by selling proprietary and third-party products from its subsidiaries in nine major European countries and via distribution agreements in an additional 10 countries.
U.K.-based biotech
Allergy Therapeutics was recently granted marketing authorization in Germany for its subcutaneous grass pollen allergen immunotherapy, Grassmuno. It works through a course of six injections, which are given before the hay fever season starts to reduce moderate to severe grass pollen allergy symptoms. According to the company, Grassmuno has the advantage of being a short course of injections, whereas other subcutaneous immunotherapy products generally require months-long treatment schedules.

The company is also developing VLP Peanut, a short-course peanut allergy vaccine candidate administered via subcutaneous injection. A phase 1/2a trial of the asset met its primary safety endpoint with an excellent safety profile up to the highest planned dose; preliminary data from 48 participants demonstrated that a 2000-fold increase in the dose of VLP Peanut was safe and well tolerated. Biomarker data in peanut allergy patients also demonstrated a strong immunomodulating response. According to the company, plans are now underway to prepare an investigational medicinal product for a phase 2b trial, with dosing of VLP Peanut being informed by the combined safety and efficacy information generated during the phase 1/2a trial.

Dedicated to revolutionising the treatment of food allergies by developing next-generation, disease-modifying immunotherapies that are safer and more convenient than existing approaches, Aravax is currently focused on the development of its lead candidate, PVX108.

The asset is an advanced immunotherapy designed to “re-train” the immune system by administering engineered peptides to precisely target T cells and reverse the course of allergic disease. Additionally, unlike most other treatments, PVX108 does not contain peanut proteins, which can potentially put patients at significant risk of serious side effects, leading to complex and burdensome dosing regimens.

Since closing a $42 million series B round in January 2024, Aravax has been progressing PVX108 through phase 2 of development. In October that same year, the allergy company announced that it had completed recruitment in its phase 2 trial of the candidate. The study, conducted under a U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) Investigational New Drug Application, enrolled 95 patients across eight trial sites in the U.S. and six in Australia. Trial participants were children and adolescents aged four to 17 years old with a peanut allergy.

In phase 1 trials, researchers observed that PVX108 produced relevant changes in the immune response to peanut protein, which continued to develop once dosing had completed. Meanwhile, the primary endpoint of the phase 2 study is the ratio of the maximum tolerated dose of peanut protein in a controlled food challenge at the end of the study period relative to baseline. Headline results are expected in H1 2026, and the company has strengthened its board in recent times in preparation for taking PVX108 into phase 3.

French biotech company DBV Technologies is advancing epicutaneous immunotherapy – which aims to re-educate the immune system of patients through the skin – using its VIASKIN patch technology to develop treatment options for immunologic diseases with significant unmet medical needs. The company currently has a pipeline of allergy candidates that it says could potentially transform the care of food-allergic patients.
French biotech company
The company’s most advanced product candidate is the VIASKIN peanut patch. In December 2025, DBV announced positive topline results from a phase 3 trial of the asset in peanut-allergic children aged four to seven years. The study met its primary endpoint as the patch demonstrated a statistically significant treatment effect, with 46.6% of children in the VIASKIN Peanut arm meeting the treatment responder criteria after 12 months, as compared to 14.8% of children in the placebo arm, exceeding the lower bound prespecified threshold of 15%. DBV is now moving forward with plans for a Biologics License Application (BLA) submission in the U.S. in the first half of this year.
In March 2025, the allergy company announced a financing of up to $306.9 million (€284.5 million), which it said at the time would go toward advancing the VIASKIN peanut patch through BLA submission and U.S. commercial launch if the product is approved.

With a single-cell RNA-sequencing-based discovery platform called SEQ SIFTER, IgGenix captures extremely rare human B cells that produce IgE antibodies directly from human blood. Through its technology, the company then isolates and re-engineers fully human, high-affinity, allergen-specific IgG antibodies designed to block and prevent the allergic cascade. By targeting common immunodominant antigens and their most immunodominant epitopes across patients, IgGenix intends to treat both pediatric and adult patients.
The allergy company’s lead candidate, IGNX001, is a human IgG4 monoclonal antibody being developed for peanut allergies and is designed to neutralize the most clinically important peanut allergens and epitopes. The company completed enrollment in a phase 1 trial of the candidate in September 2025. According to the NIH clinical trials website , the study was completed in November 2025 – although IgGenix has not yet posted the results.

Inimmune’s focus is to discover and develop disease-modifying immunotherapies. It currently has a pipeline of two clinical-stage Toll-like receptor (TLR) agonists in both immuno-oncology and allergy. The company’s allergy therapy, INI-2004, is its most advanced candidate.
INI-2004 is an allergen-agnostic immunotherapy that binds and activates toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4). Inimmune announced in March that it had completed enrollment and dosed the first patient in a phase 2 trial of the candidate in patients with allergic rhinitis. In a prior phase 1 study, in response to the ragweed challenge, participants treated intranasally with INI-2004 showed a dose-dependent improvement in nasal congestion as measured by acoustic rhinometry compared to placebo participants. Topline data from the phase 2 trial is expected in June 2026.
The company is also investigating INI-2004 in food allergies via a 2024 collaboration with Intrommune, which is evaluating whether the addition of INI-2004 to Intrommune’s INT301 can enhance the desensitization response in preclinical models for peanut allergies. According to the press release, the combination of these two approaches is expected to result in the rapid and long-term relief of allergic symptoms resulting from peanut exposure and would be a game-changer for patients suffering from this allergy.

This delivers allergenic proteins to areas of the oral cavity, potentially driving the immune system toward tolerance without ingesting the allergen.
